issue_title: Fault tollerancy of a parser.
issue_body:
Hi, I was testing the library for some of the turkish bank.
The problem with them is that that they a not really well formed (according to the standard). 
Many banks, in general, don't really follow well the standard.
I was wondering if I can work on making a PR which makes the parser more fault-tolerant?
So the parser will give his best effort to parse the entry, but if it fails it does not return an error just the fields which it was able to parse.
One of the examples of best effort when one of the "mandatory" fields Is missing like field  28C ("Statement Number/Sequence Number").

issue_title: gabbi can't code with an ipv6 host argument
issue_body: If an ipv6 address is passed in host when doing `build_tests` the built url is incorrect. An ipv6 address in a url is supposed to be bracketed: `[::1]`.

issue_title: 为什么不给posseg添加cut_for_search?
async is now a keywordand breaks pylama in Python 3.7
issue_body:
pylama has an argparse option called `async`, in Python 3.7 async is now a keyword,

Line 10 of of `main.py` throws an error

```python
from .async import check_async
```

and line 46

```python
if options.async:
```

and finally line 202 of `config.py`

```python
if options.async and 'pylint' in options.linters:
```

are three references I found that caused pylama to break for me on Python 3.7. I don't use the `--async` option so I just removed all those references locally to hack in a fix.

CC @pkittenis

issue_title: Array comparisons return a single boolean if arrays are 0
issue_body:
When I compare two numpy array quantities that are both all zeros, I get a single boolean value, but would expect to get an *array* of booleans. **Steps to reproduce** ```python x = np.zeros(4) * ureg.meter y = np.zeros(4) * ureg.meter return x==y ``` returns `True` **Expected behavior**
As described in #75, the return value should be `array([ True, True, True, True], dtype=bool)` We get the expected behavior if the arrays are non-zero, e.g., ```python x = np.ones(4) * ureg.meter y = np.ones(4) * ureg.meter x==y ``` This is with Pint 0.7.2 installed from PyPI, Python 2.7.6 [GCC 4.8.2] installed with apt-get on Ubuntu 14.04. I've also observed this with pint 0.6.

issue_title: Stop hardcoding email_validator==1.0.2 requirement
Different results when running unit tests using Faker one by one and all together
issue_body:
When I run tests on their own, the data differs than when running the entire test module. Introducing an inability to run tests on their own seems unwise. What am I missing about using this library in tests?

```python
from faker import Faker
from unittest import TestCase

fake = Faker()
fake.seed(1)


class FakerTestCase(TestCase):
    def test_01(self):
        n = fake.name()
        assert n == 'Ryan Gallagher', n
        # when running this test on its own, it passes
        # when running the entire module, it also passes since it's the first one to be executed

    def test_02(self):
        n = fake.name()
        assert n == 'Ryan Gallagher', n
        # when running this test on its own, it passes
        # when running the entire module, it fails. n is always "Jon Cole"

    def test_03(self):
        n = fake.name()
        assert n == 'Ryan Gallagher', n
        # when running this test on its own, it passes
        # when running the entire module, it fails. n is always "Rachel Davis"
```

The test runner is nosetests. As you can see, nothing fancy is going on, like test execution in random order.

issue_title: [patch] Allow pcap.Reader to work with StringIO objects.
issue_body:
_From [george.m...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/114956796509061408799/) on November 15, 2013 10:12:38_

What steps will reproduce the problem? 1. Pass a StringIO object to dpkt.pcap.Reader() What is the expected output? What do you see instead? This should parse the file, but instead it raises AttributeError because StringIO doesn't have a fileno attribute. What version of the product are you using? On what operating system? dpkt 1.8 on Ubuntu 12.04 Please provide any additional information below. I've attached a patch to fix this problem.  I don't call .fileno() on the passed in fileobj until someone calls .fileno() on the Reader object.

**Attachment:** [0001-Allow-pcap.Reader-to-work-on-file-like-objects-witho.patch](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=117)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=117_

Any chance we can get version 0.8 pushed out? There's been significant updates since 2004 (I'm looking at you, https://github.com/kmike/datrie/commit/4bb5493ce269cc0773afc15a1a3ee2a677c32757). 

Appreciate you guys working on this. It's by far the best write-enabled trie implementation.

issue_title: git push to branch not of same name: use git's suggestion
issue_body:
I checked out a branch with a different name than the remote counterpart:

```
git checkout -b local-branch myupstream/remote-branch
```

When I attempted to push my changes upstream, I got the following error:
```
$ git push -f
fatal: The upstream branch of your current branch does not match
the name of your current branch.  To push to the upstream branch
on the remote, use

    git push myupstream HEAD:remote-branch

To push to the branch of the same name on the remote, use

    git push myupstream local-branch
```

However, thefuck didn't use this information to provide a suggestion:
```
$ fuck
git push -d [enter/↑/↓/ctrl+c] 
```

issue_title: Add ability to switch between "develop" locations (often local) for a dependency and the preferred (often remote) location
issue_body: The idea would be you could optionally specify a `develop` option for dependency and then, when `--develop` is passed on the command line, or read from local configuration, that location would then be used. This really only allows seamless switching between the `develop` and preferred `location` and the use case is really to allow you to develop a lot of inter-dependent repositories at the same time and switch between local and remote code. You can achieve this today be simply locally overriding the the relevant `--<dependency>-location` setting and saving in your local conf file, but this feature should make things a little easier and allow a default `develop` path to be specified in your `scontruct` file.

issue_title: Support GitLab-CI
issue_body:
Looks like the [GitLab-CI](https://about.gitlab.com/gitlab-ci/) is having the same issues that [Appveyor had with colors](http://help.appveyor.com/discussions/suggestions/197-support-ansi-color-codes).

If `green` is run within the gitlab-runner instance, then it should turn **off** Colorama so that colors are enabled.

I have no idea how you'd check for that, though... When the runner is installed on a Windows machine, it runs in a Windows terminal...


I guess the best way to support it would be to have a CLI option of "do not allow Colorama to convert color codes" or something like that.

issue_body:
I have an issue when querying an Active Directory server.
Older ldap3 versions works, bisection shows the issue appearing between ldap3 0.9.8.8 and 0.9.9.

When the query hits an user located in a another domain, ldap3 hits a KeyError exception.
```text
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\core\connection.py", line 614, in search
    response = self.post_send_search(self.send('searchRequest', request, controls))
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\sync.py", line 142, in post_send_search
    responses, result = self.get_response(message_id)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\base.py", line 301, in get_response
    responses = self._get_response(message_id)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\sync.py", line 167, in _get_response
    ldap_resp = decode_message_fast(response)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 72, in decode_message_fast
    decoded = decode_sequence(message, ber_value_offset, ber_len + ber_value_offset, LDAP_MESSAGE_CONTEXT)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 92, in decode_sequence
    value = ber_decoder(message, start, start + ber_len, context_decoders)  # call value decode function
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 94, in decode_sequence
    value = context_decoders[ber_type](message, start, start + ber_len)  # call value decode function for context class
KeyError: 3
```
I could make it work again by simply decoding the message using decode_octet_string.
Anonymized, the message looks like this:
```python
server == "local.domain.name"

ber_class == 2
ber_type == 3
value == b'\x04oldaps://other.domain.name/OU=Users,DC=other,DC=domain,DC=name'
```


```patch
--- a/ldap3/utils/asn1.py
+++ b/ldap3/utils/asn1.py
@@ -90,8 +90,10 @@ def decode_sequence(message, start, stop, context_decoders=None):
         start += ber_value_offset
         if ber_decoder:
             value = ber_decoder(message, start, start + ber_len, context_decoders)  # call value decode function
-        else:
+        elif ber_type in context_decoders:
             value = context_decoders[ber_type](message, start, start + ber_len)  # call value decode function for context class
+        else:
+            value = decode_octet_string(message, start, start + ber_len)
         decoded.append((ber_class, ber_constructed, ber_type, value))
         start += ber_len
 
```

issue_title: Ubuntu, pip install dlib (common.h:111:20: fatal error: Python.h: No such file or directory)
Switch Python Binding to pybind11 Instead of Boost.Python
issue_body:
I've recently found out about [pybind11](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11), which is essentially a header only reimplementation of Boost.Python with C++11, and without any dependency on boost.  It also has superior documentation compared to Boost.Python, and while it's API is very similar to Boost.Python it does seem a little cleaner.  

Over the last year, it seems like the vast majority of people complaining about difficulties building dlib have actually been complaining about difficulties installing/building boost.  So it would be great to get rid of Boost.Python and use pybind11.  It doesn't look like too much work since the pybind11 API is very similar to Boost.Python.

issue_title: Suggestion: Remove try...catch on the import of tkinter in the controls
issue_body: Each control has a try...catch to handle failure to import tkinter. However, this is handled in the App and Box classes, whose instances are passed to the controls. To save the expense on performance, having try...catch in the controls should not be necessary for this, if already checked when initiating it in the respective container classes.

issue_title: Sequence type and nested types are not handled correctly.
issue_body: Sequences do not trigger an exception if their contents are of incorrect type, i.e. any sequence is always a sequence and content type checks are ignored. This is a limitations of a current type parser and validator. This should be resolved when a new parser ( #2  ) is implemented.

issue_title: Migrating over community-driven project
issue_body:
To everyone interested,
in this last weeks I tried to contact the author of the library in order to let the project move on again.
This was due to the fact that the author itself hasn't accepted a single PR or committed a line from the 6th of march of this year, and the code itself began to age (no CI tools integration with 3.4/3.5, to cite one). Anyhow the contact attempt failed, and I never got a feedback.
What I thought now, after a [brief discussion](https://github.com/dbader/schedule/pull/102) with @laubed was that maybe the project could continue in another repository, possibly keeping the fork with the original project and possibly having more than one contributor in order to ensure that any author defections will never let the project lag behind.

I'm surely still hoping that the author will return, and if he will we also pull the forked version over his. 
Please, tell me if you see any other solution than this, otherwise we'll organize a place for discussing more deeply the topic.

issue_title: Package permissions issue
issue_body:
I'm having a weird issue that, to be honest, I can't imagine how it's a bincopy issue but it doesn't appear to affect any other packages.  When our admin bootstraps a Windows machine he installs bincopy (and several other packages).  Later, a user runs python and attempts to run `import bincopy` and gets an `ImportError`.  Logging in as admin, I can successfully perform the import.  

We suspected it was a permissions error so we tested our theory by changing the owner of the Libs directory to the present user (recursively) and ensuring proper permissions were set, but it made no difference.  We then logged in as admin and removed bincopy (`pip uninstall bincopy`), then reinstalled as the user account (`pip install bincopy`) and everything worked fine.  In both cases bincopy was installed into `C:\Python27\Libs\site-packages`.

I looked through the package and can't find anything that should be affected by installing user.  Have you seen anything like this?  I have been installing and using bincopy under Linux for a while without any such issues.

issue_title: Gdspy 1.1 write_gds() for hierarchical files
issue_body:
@heitzmann I have installed the latest release (Patch 1.1.1) on Mac OSX, and am observing a different behaviour for the GDSII writing functionality compared to previous releases. In the past (release 1.0), `gds_print()` would accept a list of cells to be included in the output file, and then append any cell dependencies to the list of cells to be written. Here's the relevant code: 
if cells == None:
        cells = iter(Cell.cell_dict.values())
    else:
        cells = [Cell.cell_dict.get(c, c) for c in cells]
        i = 0
        while i < len(cells):
            for cell in cells[i].get_dependencies():
                if cell not in cells:
                    cells.append(cell)
            i += 1
        ...
        for cell in cells:
             outfile.write(cell.to_gds(unit / precision))
This worked flawlessly for deeply hierarchical cell structures. The implementation of this functionality in 1.1.1 (`write_gds()` function), however, does not explicitly add the dependencies: 
if cells is None:
        cells = self.cell_dict.values()
    else:
        cells = [self.cell_dict.get(c, c) for c in cells]
        for cell in cells:
             outfile.write(cell.to_gds(unit / precision))
resulting in an empty GDSII file. If I add the while / for block (the part in bold above), the dependencies are indeed added, and the resulting file contains all intended hierarchy. Should the dependencies be explicitly added here, as before? Or am I missing something in the new implementation of `GdsLibrary`?

issue_title: UnicodeEncodeError under Python 3 if request body contains URL-encoded non-ASCII chars
issue_body:
If all of the following are true:
* Python 3
* `match_on` contains `body`
* request body is URL-encoded non-ASCII string
* attempting to replay a previously recorded cassette

then VCR.py blows up with "UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character '\xe9' in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)" (full stack trace below).

Here's a demo script:
```
$ cat unicode-body.py
from __future__ import print_function
from six.moves import urllib
import vcr

myvcr = vcr.VCR(match_on=('method', 'scheme', 'host', 'port', 'path', 'query', 'body'))

params = {b'foo': u'\u00e9'.encode('utf-8')}

issue_title: Send() method used without checking that all data was sent
issue_body:
The [send()](http://paramiko-docs.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/channel.html#paramiko.channel.Channel.send) method of Paramiko channels doesn't guarantee that all the data gets sent, but there is a [sendall()](http://paramiko-docs.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/channel.html#paramiko.channel.Channel.sendall) method that does. Using sendall() saves you from having to write your own loop.

This is an easy swap - I'll submit a PR for review.

issue_title: Corrupted Queue in Python's multiprocessing Pool implementation
issue_body:
[Python's multiprocessing pool](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html) has various limitations, one I tried to solve with my wrapper code is the [timeout of worker processes](https://github.com/andresriancho/w3af/blob/master/w3af/core/data/parsers/mp_document_parser.py#L126). I implemented that in a rather ugly way, when the timeout is reached I `os.kill` the process. The multiprocessing pool implementation does spawn a new worker process and 99.99% of the time everything works well.

0.01% of the time there are some strange issues and [the whole pool stops working](https://github.com/andresriancho/w3af/issues/9713), which I believe is because of this issue which is documented in the python docs:

> Warning If a process is killed using Process.terminate() or os.kill() while it is trying to use a Queue, then the data in the queue is likely to become corrupted. This may cause any other process to get an exception when it tries to use the queue later on.

While looking for different multiprocessing pool implementations I found yours, which does implement timeouts and as far as I could read from [process.py#L360-L366](https://github.com/noxdafox/pebble/blob/master/pebble/pool/process.py#L360-L366) there is code to avoid killing a process when the queue is locked. Am I reading that part of the code correctly?

Which other issues from python's multiprocessing pool did you fix in your implementation?

Does your pool implementation have any known issues?

issue_title: Resampling of integer channels
issue_body:
Resampling integer channels should not be done with linear interpolation (np.interp). Consider using scipy.interpolate.interp1d(kind='zero') instead. This could be hardcoded, or, if more flexibility is desired, give the user the option to specify zero order interpolation for integer signals.

The downside is that it would introduce an extra dependency on scipy in the mdf module.

issue_title: Unit testing needs a lot of work
issue_body:
I mostly use graphs to 'eyeball' how things are working. Tthere are well known performance bounds, such as Cramer-Rao, for these filters. I need to think through what I want re unit testing and turn it into a PR. FilterPy also probably needs supporting functions -  if I can turn a Cramer-Rao into a stand alone function it should be part of the general library, for example.

There are better bounds than Cramer-Rao, but they can be a bear to compute. 

 It occurs to me that I don't test things like immutability of things like self.F. What if I force an exception - are things in a standard state? Etc. All pameter inputs need to be tested. And so on.

Anyway, it is time to think through a solid testing plan, set some reasonable standards for what tests must be done for any functionality, and then execute on at least some of it. 

This is important - this was designed largely as a pedagogical tool, but Ph.D students are using it in their research, some companies are using it for there work - there are real-world consequences to bugs.

issue_title: Silent evaluation failures due to too low recursion-limit(100)
issue_body:
While evaluating *correct* expressions, I got `None` as result, but no error were reported (they have silently failed).

When I tried to investigate the issue, I was receiving the following error on `Interpreter().eval()` from within my debugger (LiClipse + PyDev):

    cannot set the recursion limit to 100 at the recursion depth 87: the limit is too low

Obviously my debugger had pushed the stack close to that limit, 
and python-interpreter screamed at [asteval/asteval.py#L136](https://github.com/newville/asteval/blob/master/asteval/asteval.py#L136): 

    sys.setrecursionlimit(RECURSION_LIMIT)

So I hand-edited [asteval/astutils.py:#L16](https://github.com/newville/asteval/blob/master/asteval/astutils.py#L16) and increased my limit to i.e. 500: 

     RECURSION_LIMIT = 100

But then, all my expressions worked OK!

On first impression, I would consider `recursion-limit=100`, too low for general use.

My second thought would be to set the limit at least  `current_stack_depth + 100` and that value(100) to be user-configurable.

But as general precaution, I believe that:

- normal code should not mess with `sys.setrecursionlimit()` because it might have serious side-effects.  
- If this protection measure is needed, user-code should perform that invocation - the documents should explain how.  
- At most, an "opt-in" keyword should do that, with something like that:

  ```
  import inspect
  
  class Interpreter:
      def __init__(..., recursion_cap=None):
          ...
          if recursion_cap:
              if recursion_cap is True:
                  recursion_cap = DEFAULT_RECURSION_CAP
              recursion_limit = len(inspect.stack()) + recursion_cap
              sys.setrecursionlimit(recursion_limit)

  ```

issue_title: Handle TeletexString as iso-8859-1
issue_body:
So I came across a pkcs7 signature that has a x509 `common_name` that includes a `{` and was generated my m2crypto where asn1crypto chokes.

M2Crypto simply forwards asn1 encoding to openssl and I found https://wiki.openssl.org/index.php/Manual:Req(1)#BUGS which states that OpenSSL encodes TeletexString as ISO-8859-1 (Latin 1). Then I found https://github.com/etingof/pyasn1/blob/master/pyasn1/type/char.py#L241-L250 which kinda verifies that and wondered if we could implement a fallback in asn1crypto that falls back to latin-1 if we run into a decoding problem? Or, maybe even kick out T61 and just use latin-1?

See https://github.com/mozilla/signing-clients/pull/24 the signature in question.

`discord.Embed(**kwargs)`
Represents a Discord embed.
The following attributes can be set during creation of the object:
Certain properties return an `EmbedProxy`. Which is a type that acts similar to a regular dict except access the attributes via dotted access, e.g. `embed.author.icon_url`. If the attribute is invalid or `empty`, then a special sentinel value is returned, `Embed.Empty`.
For ease of use, all parameters that expect a str are implicitly casted to str for you.

The list of Message.embeds isn't a list of discord.Embed ? if not why ?

    ctx = yield from self.get_context(message)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/discord/ext/commands/bot.py", line 847, in get_context
    invoked_prefix = discord.utils.find(view.skip_string, prefix)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/discord/utils.py", line 167, in find
    if predicate(element):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/discord/ext/commands/view.py", line 62, in skip_string
    strlen = len(string)
TypeError: object of type 'NoneType' has no len()
```
This is undeniably an unhelpful exception. and proposed fixes are as follows:
1. When `None` is presented, simply ignore it. (this means that if `command_prefix` is `None` or any sequence of just `None`s (e.g. `[None]`) commands will not function at all.)
2. Raise whenever `None` is present (this is effectively the same as what is already happening, but can be used to have a more helpful error message)
3. Raise on `None` only if there are no alternatives present (e.g. `None` would raise but `[None, '?']` would not)
4. Ignore `None` as in 1 but use `warnings` to warn the user a la `utils.deprecated` (alternatively, you could use `logging` at WARN level)

Will update this issue if, and when, PR or fix is made.

issue_title: Servers with streaming endpoints that redirect to a different URL don't work
issue_body:
This one is tough and I don't know the best way to handle it, but...

Basically, Mastodon redirects things under /api/v1/streaming to a different URL (via 301) when configured to do so, due to the nature of various setups.

But it **only** redirects things under that endpoint, sub-endpoints like /api/v1/streaming/public return 404 instantly.

Worse still, requests strips the Authorization header when redirecting.

So, the right thing to determine the streaming endpoint:

1) Hit /api/v1/streaming to see if you get a 301.
2) If you don't, you can stop here, otherwise, continue.
3) Hit the URL referenced by the Location header. This is the endpoint root.
4) All requests for the streaming server must be redirected to this URL base from now on.

I'll try to implement it, meanwhile I'm working on server-side workarounds for Mastodon.

issue_body:
I believe the current configuration file format is equivalent to the .ini format that is readable in python using [ConfigParser](https://docs.python.org/2/library/configparser.html).  So I could probably replace my manual config parsing stuff with that.

issue_title: zebra stripes don't get applied if TABLE is contained in DIV with style=display:none
issue_body:
Thank you for the work on tablesorter. I have a table inside a div inside another div:
...
The inner div will initially have display:none if the outer div has the class "closed", in which case the whole table will not be visible. zebra stripes are working OK only if the table is not contained in invisible elements while tablesortifying. Ideally, the zebra classes should still be applied if the table is contained in invisible elements. I think this is caused by the way jQuery's CSS selector extension ":visible" is used in the logic that finds the rows that should get applied the zebra classes in jquery.tablesorter.js:2728 : $visibleRows = $tbodies.eq( tbodyIndex ).children( 'tr:visible' ).not( c.selectorRemove ); Probably not too hard to fix, my time and knowledge of jQuery and tablesorter's internals is lacking too much though. Perhaps I could trigger an "applyWidgets" event to all tablesortified tables contained in the div when the div becomes visible but I feel like that would be a dirty workaround. Not critical for me right now, after all it's just an UI thing. The other widgets I'm using here (saveSort, filter, resizable, stickyHeaders) seem to be initialized OK when the table is contained in an invisible element while tablesortifying.

issue_title: complex hessian matrix
issue_body:
Hi,
I went through the following issue recently while trying to compute the Hessian matrix of a cost function which outputs complex numbers. Note that there is no similar problem while evaluating the gradient.
numdifftools casts the imaginary part and return a real matrix. I made a dirty fix in forcing the `hess` variable in line 943 of core.py to be complex and it does work, so I guess it should be easy to correct.

Here is a toy example to reproduce the problem:
```
import numpy as np
import numdifftools as nd

def foo(x):
    return np.inner(x, x)-1j

x = np.random.randn(3)
hessn = nd.Hessian(foo)

hessn(x)
```
which outputs in my case:
`C:\MyPrograms\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\numdifftools\core.py:947: ComplexWarning: Casting complex values to real discards the imaginary part
  ) / (4. * hess[i, i])`

Thanks a lot for this great tool that you provide!

issue_title: Question: should validation be different for signature creation vs. validation?
issue_body:
There is code present that is present for the purpose of supporting non-standard or legacy transactions. Here are some examples:
- https://github.com/richardkiss/pycoin/blob/b2dd44f5674913cae08a4079c76797b6d3bfeae4/pycoin/tx/Tx.py#L183
- https://github.com/richardkiss/pycoin/blob/b2dd44f5674913cae08a4079c76797b6d3bfeae4/pycoin/tx/script/check_signature.py#L133
- https://github.com/richardkiss/pycoin/blob/b2dd44f5674913cae08a4079c76797b6d3bfeae4/pycoin/tx/script/vm.py#L51 (see `disallow_long_scripts` argument)

There are other cases where transactions may be valid according the protocol and may make it into blocks, but probably shouldn't be relayed or be created by modern tool kits. One of the most well known examples is the pop-one-too-many bug with `OP_CHECKMULTISIG`. Technically, it doesn't matter what the value is, but modern implementations enforce `OP_0` to avoid malleability. So a transaction using (e.g.) `OP_1` instead of `OP_0` in a block should probably be treated as valid, but being relayed from a peer should probably be accepted into the memory pool, but not re-relayed.

All of this puts tool kits in an awkward position where validation of some transactions should be more lenient than others (e.g., those created by the tool kit itself).

pycoin's support for this bifurcated validation (where it exists) is somewhat ad hoc. In fairness, even in `bitcoind`, there's an awkward `flags` argument that is used for this purpose (see [here](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/fcf646c9b08e7f846d6c99314f937ace50809d7a/src/script/standard.h#L32) and [here](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/41e6e4caba9899ce7c165b0784461c55c867ee24/src/script/interpreter.cpp#L238)).

I'm opening this issue merely to open the discussion regarding:
1. Whether this should be addressed at all; and
2. How best to do it.

issue_title: Relative pid file paths don't work
issue_body:
Currently daemonize will gladly accept a relative path to a pid file, then chdir to "/" and then throw an exception when it can't remove the now invalid pid file path.

A simple patch to make this work is to save the absolute path of the pid file on instantiation.
```diff
--- "..\\envs\\mn\\Lib\\site-packages\\daemonize.py"
+++ "plusdashfilter\\daemonize.py"
@@ -44,6 +44,7 @@
                  user=None, group=None, verbose=False, logger=None,
                  foreground=False, chdir="/"):
         self.app = app
+        self._pid = None
         self.pid = pid
         self.action = action
         self.keep_fds = keep_fds or []
@@ -56,6 +57,14 @@
         self.foreground = foreground
         self.chdir = chdir
 
+    @property
+    def pid(self):
+        return self._pid
+
+    @pid.setter
+    def pid(self, pid):
+        self._pid = os.path.abspath(pid)
+
     def sigterm(self, signum, frame):
         """
         These actions will be done after SIGTERM.
```

I modified my bird6.conf import policy for peer:
From:
   if net !~ [2000::/3+] then
To:

issue_title: Issue: Adding carriage return to URL in a column results in corrupt xlsx file
issue_body:
For certain URLs, an extra '\n' at the end can results in a corrupt xlsx file, which excel complains about and asks permission to recover. 

This is using Python version : 2.7 (on Windows 7)

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:

```python

import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('demo3.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.write('A1', 'URL_Or_Path')
worksheet.write('A2', 'private/var/log/lamp.log')
worksheet.write('A3', 'smb://192.168.0.12:7777')
worksheet.write('A4', 'smb://readyshare')
worksheet.write('A5', 'ftp://192.168.191.193\n') # <--Problem code
worksheet.write('A6', 'smb://saturn')

workbook.close()
```
Resultant file:
[demo3.xlsx](https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/files/1223694/demo3.xlsx)

issue_title: Content-Length header leaks original length of placeholder values
issue_body:
Since we record all the headers, even when we replace sensitive tokens with their placeholders, the original total length of all tokens is preserved.

A few possible options:

1. Fake the content-length by incrementing by `placeholder length - original length` when using placeholders bidirectionally
2. Simply drop `Content-Length` when placeholders are used?
3. Keep current behavior but note the risk in the docs

issue_title: Blocking=False causes error.
issue_body:
When passing `blocking=False` to semaphore - instead of acquiring the lock, I get `IndexError`:

```
In [1]: from redis_semaphore import Semaphore

In [2]: from onrecruit.concurrency import redis_connection

In [3]: s1 = Semaphore(redis_connection, count=1, namespace='aasdfasdfasdf', blocking=False)

In [4]: s1.acquire()
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IndexError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-4-477f1a8eb196> in <module>()
----> 1 s1.acquire()

/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/redis_semaphore/__init__.py in acquire(self, timeout, target)
     59                 raise NotAvailable
     60 
---> 61         token = pair[1]
     62         self._local_tokens.append(token)
     63         self.client.hset(self.grabbed_key, token, self.current_time)

IndexError: index out of range

In [5]: s1.acquire()
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
NotAvailable                              Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-5-477f1a8eb196> in <module>()
----> 1 s1.acquire()

/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/redis_semaphore/__init__.py in acquire(self, timeout, target)
     57             pair = self.client.lpop(self.available_key)
     58             if pair is None:
---> 59                 raise NotAvailable
     60 
     61         token = pair[1]
```

From this point on lock is always acquired (it always raises `NotAvailable`) and you cannot `release()` it. Only `reset()` helps.

Tested on python 3.5, with redis 2.8 and 3.0 (same behaviour), Redis-Semaphore==0.2.0 and redis==2.10.5

issue_title: Change of the pagination template
Integrity errors can be generated if 'id' is returned by get_user_details()
issue_body:
If you have a response like the following:

```python
reponse = {
  'id': 2,
  'email': 'user@example.com',
  'username': 'testuser',
  'first_name': 'test',
  'last_name': 'user',
}
```
And a custom OAuthBackend like below:

```python
class MyAuthBackend(OAuthBackend):

    def get_user_details(self, response):
        return response
    ...
```

An IntegrityError can be thrown if the Django user created has an id different to the id in the response or existing data could be overridden. 

This is because `social_auth.backends.pipeline.user.update_user_details()` blindly writes all the fields returned by `get_user_details()` (except username) to the User object. In this case the `id` will overwrite the existing `id` causing the User object to insert a new record (causing the IntegrityError) or overwrite another record.

I think that `update_user_details()` should filter for `id` and `pk` along with `username` to avoid this from happening.

I think this is better than just telling developers to not return `id` or `pk` from `get_user_details` because it is hard to debug and because a method called `update_user_details()` really shouldn't be touching these fields - even if they are set.

issue_title: Allow headers to differ from fieldnames
issue_body:
In `DictWriter` the header is currently defined as:

```python

This means the keys in the rows have to match the headers of the file. But that's inconvenient because the keys are often things like `dateOfOrder` whereas we'd like a human-readable header in the CSV file: "Date of order".

Hi Johannes - thanks for an elegant implementation of these perceptual hash algorithms.

A performance enhancement suggestion for hash comparison (_sub_):  

My understanding is that:

self.hash.flatten() != other.hash.flatten()

will do a boolean compare for each element in the hash array

Optimising this via the clever bitCount() below could be useful when comparing large hash sets 
from: https://wiki.python.org/moin/BitManipulation

def bitCount(int_type):
       count = 0
       while(int_type):
       int_type &= int_type - 1
       count += 1
       return(count)

issue_title: Popen Failing due to invalid _make_args
issue_body:
I'm using the 2.3.1 release version running on Python 3.6.1 64-bit. This issue is probably related to issue #161 and #144. 

I noticed when debugging that the **_make_args()** function in **_meta.py** is not generating command line arguments correctly, because it is stripping **self.binary** of path separators on Windows.

`cmd = shlex.split(' '.join(self._make_args(args, passphrase)))`

This is the resulting array from that command:
`<class 'list'>: ['C:gnupggpg.exe', '--no-options', '--no-emit-version', '--no-tty', '--status-fd', '2', '--homedir', "'C:\\Users\\tuxpr\\AppData\\Roaming\\gnupg\\'", '--no-default-keyring', '--keyring', 'C:\\Users\\tuxpr\\AppData\\Roaming\\gnupg\\pubring.gpg', '--secret-keyring', 'C:\\Users\\tuxpr\\AppData\\Roaming\\gnupg\\secring.gpg', '--no-use-agent', '--debug-all', '--debug-level=basic', '--list-config', '--with-colons']`

Notice the first element, 'C:gnupggpg.exe' should be 'C:\\gnupg\\gpg.exe'. I can manually inject that value during debug, but there is still another problem with the control of the Popen call.

Spaces present an entirely new problem. Here is another cmd array:
`<class 'list'>: ['C:Program', 'Files', '(x86)GnuPGbingpg.exe', '--no-options', '--no-emit-version', '--no-tty', '--status-fd', '2', '--homedir', "'C:\\Users\\tuxpr\\AppData\\Roaming\\gnupg\\'", '--no-default-keyring', '--keyring', 'C:\\Users\\tuxpr\\AppData\\Roaming\\gnupg\\pubring.gpg', '--secret-keyring', 'C:\\Users\\tuxpr\\AppData\\Roaming\\gnupg\\secring.gpg', '--no-use-agent', '--debug-all', '--debug-level=basic', '--list-config', '--with-colons']`

The path for that call is supposed to be "C:\\Program Files (x86)\\GnuPG\\bin\\gpg.exe", but it seems to be splitting on each space, and stripping the path separators.

After Popen is called, you must call **proc.wait()** in **_check_sane_and_get_gpg_version** before reading any values back from the subprocess, otherwise no execution has occurred.

After **proc.wait()** returns, you can then access the **proc.returncode** and read output data from the process. If **proc.wait()** hangs, then gpg has been invoked but doesn't understand the arguments given, and is waiting for user input by default.

Hope this helps someone on the code side of things. I'm not familiar enough with the way that **shlex.split()** works or I'd write it myself.

issue_title: Feature Request/Estimate - nested_fields as a tuple instead of a Boolean
issue_body:
Hi @eamigo86 , thanks for this great library!!!

Can I make a suggestion?

For our mutations, we have some fields that we would like to pass the Foreign Key ID as an update instead of creating a new object, and some fields that we would like to create a new object through a nested structure. In other words, for some fields we want the result of `nested_fields` = True (nested object creation), and other fields we want the result of `nested_fields` = False (IDs passed in).

Do you think it would be better instead of passing a boolean for the nested_fields, pass a list of fields, to allow only nesting the fields that you specify in DjangoSerializerMutation? Similar to `only_fields`?

For example, below for the Foreign Key of Project and the related_name of "products":

```
class RoomSerializerMutation(DjangoSerializerMutation):

    class Meta:
        serializer_class = RoomSerializer
        nested_fields = ("project", "products", )
```
All other Foreign Key fields (including Many to Many, One to One, etc.) would take in an ID.

If you agree, I also wanted to get a general understanding of how much time that would take as well. I forked the repo and have been experimented with it to see what I can do to provide this feature myself, but I'm not very confident that I can do it since the codebase is so large and I am just learning it.

Thanks again!

issue_title: Log records get lost
issue_body:
Hi,

I face the problem that some log messages are not sent to Elasticsearch. The reason seems to be that the buffer is not thread safe because `flush` is called concurrently by the flush timer. As a result, when the flush timer calls `flush`, there is a good chance that log records that were appended to the buffer by `emit` in the meantime get lost.
Disabling the timer might solve the problem. But it has to be ensured that log messages from the elasticsearch library (which are generated during `flush`) are filtered, otherwise a _maximum recursion depth exceeded_ exception can occur.

issue_title: please switch from py2-ipaddress to ipaddress
issue_body:
Many Python distributions now have problems with pip's new strict security requirements and so end up following the steps here:

http://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html#insecureplatformwarning

Sadly, one of these pulls in this backport as a dependency:

https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ipaddress

Sadly, this is incompatible with the backport you are using:

https://pypi.python.org/pypi/py2-ipaddress

...but both distributions install an ipaddress.py, resulting in heisenbugs when the wrong package ends up being installed last.

I think some changes would be required, but it might make the world a bit of a less crazy place...

issue_title: Sharing Parameters between BucketingModules
issue_body:
## Description

I am trying to share parameters between two BucketingModules. More specifically, my requirements are:
- I have different sources of data, that are read by different data iterators
- I have several instances of `BucketingModule`. The modules have some parameters in common, and they should share them. I.e. the parameter arrays should essentially be identical.
- On top of that, the modules have other parameters that they do not share.

**What is your recommendation? How should I set up those modules?**

## Things I've tried

1) I tried defining a master module and then, while binding a second module, use the `shared_module` attribute:

https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/module/module.html?highlight=bucketing#mxnet.module.BucketingModule.bind

Unfortunately, this is not available for `BucketingModule`. (Why?)

2) It's possible to get and set params between modules to sync some of their parameters after each update. Here is a working example:

https://gist.github.com/bricksdont/3dc6213482c38526decacf2a54b7f8b6

issue_title: Consider restructuring python targets to be 1-1-1
issue_body: See #385 for the java side of this.  In java, fine grained and deep package structures are more of-the-norm.  In python, less so; so this issue is phrased as a "consider".  The idea though is to eliminate most or all exclude lists in sources globs in the python codebase.  This would require re-structuring packages and thus risk breaking clients imports.

issue_title: group.get_roles has no way to only get local_roles which breaks group.grant_roles
issue_body:
`group.get_roles(group=foo, obj=bar)` returns global roles as well as local roles. It should have the same behavior as `user.get_roles()` which has a bool argument `inherit`. When `inherit=False` it should only return local roles.

A annoying side-effect of the current behavior: When granting local roles to a group with `group.grant_roles(group=foo, roles=bar, obj=baz)` the group's global roles will be merged with the new roles and then assigned as local roles. A group with thus have their global roles also assigned as local roles. 

Opinions?

issue_title: create re-usable Cleaner class
issue_body:
While glancing at the source, the inner workings of `clean()` caught my eye.

The current structure creates 2 object instances to clean a single bit of text.  If you do a lot of cleaning to a standard configuration, a factory pattern works really well.

I tried something and set up a test suite to see if there would be a noticeable difference.  html5lib tends to be bulky and annoying, and it often lends well to caching.  

Using the factory pattern below to cache a "cleaner", we average a 15% speedup over the default `clean` if you clean more than 10 items.  if you're doing hundreds of items, the savings seems to be over 40%.

```
class _AbstractBleachCleaner(object):
    """abstract object to cache the `sanitizer` and `parser` objects, and expose a `clean` method"""
    sanitizer = None
    parser = None

    def clean(self, text):
        """cleans text using the cached parser"""
        if not text:
            return ''
        text = force_unicode(text)    
        return _render(self.parser.parseFragment(text))

def cleaner_factory( tags=ALLOWED_TAGS, attributes=ALLOWED_ATTRIBUTES,
          styles=ALLOWED_STYLES, strip=False, strip_comments=True):
    """Generates a custom bleach Cleaner that can be re-used."""
    class s(BleachSanitizer):
        allowed_elements = tags
        allowed_attributes = attributes
        allowed_css_properties = styles
        strip_disallowed_elements = strip
        strip_html_comments = strip_comments
    p = html5lib.HTMLParser(tokenizer=s)
    customCleaner = _AbstractBleachCleaner()
    customCleaner.sanitizer = s
    customCleaner.parser = p
    return customCleaner

myCleaner = cleaner_factory()
print myCleaner.clean( sample_text ) 
```

issue_title: Update run-wer-automation.sh in light of #236 and #239
Python 2 - Incorrect total # batches
issue_body:
In a [DataSet](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/util/feeding.py#L100), the total number of batches is incorrectly truncated when using Python 2.7 meaning the final batch is lost if the number of samples is not a multiple of the batch size:

```python
class DataSet(object):
    '''
    Represents a collection of audio samples and their respective transcriptions.
    Takes a set of CSV files produced by importers in /bin.
    '''
    def __init__(self, csvs, batch_size, skip=0, limit=0, ascending=True, next_index=lambda i: i + 1):
        self.batch_size = batch_size
        self.next_index = next_index
        self.files = None
        for csv in csvs:
            file = pandas.read_csv(csv, encoding='utf-8', na_filter=False)
            if self.files is None:
                self.files = file
            else:
                self.files = self.files.append(file)
        self.files = self.files.sort_values(by="wav_filesize", ascending=ascending) \
                         .ix[:, ["wav_filename", "transcript"]] \
                         .values[skip:]
        if limit > 0:
            self.files = self.files[:limit]
        self.total_batches = int(ceil(len(self.files) / batch_size))
```

Potential solution:

```python
        self.total_batches = int(ceil(float(len(self.files)) / batch_size))
```

issue_title: SignatureDoesNotMatch when s3 metadata contains two consecutive spaces
issue_body:
# Description

Including two consecutive spaces in a metadata entry triggers an invalid signature error when uploading a file to s3.

# How to reproduce
The following code shows the issue. Notice that the `'Double  Space'` string contains two spaces between the words:
```
import boto3

session = boto3.session.Session(region_name='eu-central-1',
  aws_access_key_id='***redacted***',
  aws_secret_access_key='***redacted***')

s3 = session.resource('s3')
item = s3.Object('**redacted**', 'test.pdf')
item.upload_file('test.pdf', ExtraArgs={
        'Metadata': {'key': 'Double  Space'}
})
```
Running this throws:
```
boto3.exceptions.S3UploadFailedError: Failed to upload test.pdf to **redacted**/test.pdf: An error occurred (SignatureDoesNotMatch) when calling the PutObject operation: The request signature we calculated does not match the signature you provided. Check your key and signing method.
```
However, removing one of the spaces and re-running succeeds without errors.

# Test system
- OS X 10.11.6
- Python 2.7.12 (macports compiled)
- botocore 1.5.26

issue_title: Repository crashes on bytes paths on Python 3
issue_body:
If the path supplied to `Repository` is a `bytes` objects as in Python 3, pygit2 crashes. This is inconvenient, ~~as all filesystem utilities will return bytes objects~~ they will actually return `str`s. The `os` libraries will accept `bytes` though.

Eg. as used in [powerline][0] raises `must be str, not bytes`

[0]: https://github.com/powerline/powerline/blob/develop/powerline/lib/vcs/git.py#L108

issue_title: Is there an estimate on when pip will support Pipfile?
sys.path / site.py on AWS Linux AMI
issue_body:
Please feel free to ignore/close this if it is not helpful. Am am completely new to python and had a problem installing an application that used it on an AWS Linux AMI server. Packages were installing but not recognized as installed. For example, 'pip install django' installed django successfully each time I ran it, but didn't see it as installed from the last iteration. This happened because pip installed django to lib64/pythonX.Y/dist-packages within the virtual environment but this directory was not included in sys.path so pip did not see it as installed, so installed it again. There was a mismatch between sys.path (through which pip searched for an existing installation) and the paths to which pip was installing. This could have been reconciled by either changing where the package was installed or changing sys.path so that pip could find the installed package. I opted for the latter and traced the lack of the folder in sys.path to the virtualenv site.py, produced from virtualenv.py. As changing virtualenv.py is beyond my skill with python, I created a virtual environment then edited lib64/pythonX.Y/site.py, changing function virtual_env_search_paths to 
def virtualenv_search_paths(sys_prefix, known_paths=None):
    """ Build sys.path correctly.

    $VENV/local/lib64/python3.4/site-packages
    $VENV/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages
    $VENV/lib64/python3.4
    $VENV/lib/python3.4
    $VENV/lib64/python3.4/site-packages
    $VENV/lib/python3.4/site-packages
    $VENV/lib64/python3.4/lib-dynload
    $VENV/lib/python3.4/lib-dynload
    /usr/local/lib64/python3.4/site-packages
    /usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages
    /usr/lib64/python3.4
    /usr/lib/python3.4
    /usr/lib64/python3.4/plat-linux
    /usr/lib64/python3.4/dist-packages
    /usr/lib/python3.4/dist-packages
    /usr/lib64/python3.4/lib-dynload

    This is called once with sys_prefix=$VENV (sys_prefix=sys.prefix).

    If the virtualenv is created with the --system-site-packages option,
    this is also called with sys_prefix=/usr (sys_prefix=sys.real_prefix).
    """
    if known_paths is None:
        known_paths = set()

    local_prefix = os.path.join(sys_prefix, 'local')
    prefixes = [local_prefix, sys_prefix]
    python = 'python' + sys.version[:3]

    sitedirs = []
    for prefix in prefixes:
        if prefix != local_prefix:
            # e.g. $PREFIX/lib{,64}/pythonX.Y
            add_lib_path(sitedirs, prefix, python)

        # e.g. $PREFIX/lib{,64}/pythonX.Y/site-packages
        add_lib_path(sitedirs, prefix, python, 'site-packages')

        # e.g. /usr/lib{,64}/pythonX.Y/dist-packages
        add_lib_path(sitedirs, prefix, python, 'dist-packages')

        if prefix != local_prefix:
            # e.g. $PREFIX/lib{,64}/pythonX.Y/plat-linux
            add_lib_path(sitedirs, prefix, python, 'plat-{platform}'.format(platform=sys.platform))

        if prefix != local_prefix:
            # e.g. $PREFIX/lib{,64}/pythonX.Y/lib-dynload
            add_lib_path(sitedirs, prefix, python, 'lib-dynload')

    for sitedir in sitedirs:
        if os.path.isdir(sitedir):
            addsitedir(sitedir, known_paths)
The difference from the original is: 
        # e.g. /usr/lib{,64}/pythonX.Y/dist-packages
        add_lib_path(sitedirs, prefix, python, 'dist-packages')
Which I made unconditional. This is almost certainly not a good general solution, but it was sufficient for my needs and, as I suspect many people use AWS Linux AMI based systems, I thought something along these lines might help them. Apologies if this is old news or just noise. This was: 
        if prefix == sys.real_prefix:
            # e.g. /usr/lib{,64}/pythonX.Y/dist-packages
            add_lib_path(sitedirs, prefix, python, 'dist-packages')
I note in my environment 'PYTHON_INSTALL_LAYOUT=amzn'. I don't know how this gets set but suspect it might have something to do with the inclusion of the dist-packages directories in the locations that pip installs to. In the pip code that appears to determine where the software is installed, I see some data for 'amzn'. Not wanting to interfere with system python or pip more generally, and not knowing nearly enough to understand the consequences of changing any of this, I thought best to leave these alone. Fortunately, the above noted change to site.py from virtualenv allows pip, run in a virtual environment, to see the django package it already installed and otherwise everything seems to be working OK - at least no other problems have yet made themselves obvious to me.

issue_title: Error in ssim equation in skimage.measure compare_ssim ?
issue_body:
Hi,
inside the source code for the compare_ssim function I think there is an error in the equation for the SSIM.

Currently in _structural_similarity.py the equation is defined (lines 201 onwards): 
```
A1, A2, B1, B2 = ((2 * ux * uy + C1,
                       2 * vxy + C2,
                       ux ** 2 + uy ** 2 + C1,
                       vx + vy + C2))

D = B1 * B2
S = (A1 * A2) / D

# to avoid edge effects will ignore filter radius strip around edges
    pad = (win_size - 1) // 2
# compute (weighted) mean of ssim
    mssim = crop(S, pad).mean()
```

But I think B2 has been defined incorrectly (by comparing to Wang 2004).
currently `B2 =vx + vy + C2`
But comparing back to Wang 2004 shouldn't it be: `B2=vx**2 + vy**2 + C2` ?

This makes a small different in the resulting MSSIM.

issue_title: BibCirculation: can't print  List of pending hold requests
issue_body:
Trying to print a List of pending hold requests in bibcirculation will trigger a 500. 

It is due to a missing comma in the code. 

```
def get_pdf_request_data(status):
    """
    status: request status.
    """
    res = run_sql("""SELECT DISTINCT
                            lr.id_bibrec,
                            bor.name,
                            lib.name,
                            it.location,
                            DATE_FORMAT(lr.period_of_interest_from,'%%Y-%%m-%%d'),
                            DATE_FORMAT(lr.period_of_interest_to,'%%Y-%%m-%%d'),
                            lr.request_date
                     FROM   crcLOANREQUEST lr,
                            crcBORROWER bor,
                            crcITEM it,
                            crcLIBRARY lib
                     WHERE  lr.id_crcBORROWER=bor.id AND
                            it.id_bibrec=lr.id_bibrec AND
                            lib.id = it.id_crcLIBRARY AND
                            lr.status=%s;
                  """, (status))
    return res
```

It should be : 

```
def get_pdf_request_data(status):
    """
    status: request status.
    """
    res = run_sql("""SELECT DISTINCT
                            lr.id_bibrec,
                            bor.name,
                            lib.name,
                            it.location,
                            DATE_FORMAT(lr.period_of_interest_from,'%%Y-%%m-%%d'),
                            DATE_FORMAT(lr.period_of_interest_to,'%%Y-%%m-%%d'),
                            lr.request_date
                     FROM   crcLOANREQUEST lr,
                            crcBORROWER bor,
                            crcITEM it,
                            crcLIBRARY lib
                     WHERE  lr.id_crcBORROWER=bor.id AND
                            it.id_bibrec=lr.id_bibrec AND
                            lib.id = it.id_crcLIBRARY AND
                            lr.status=%s;
                  """, (status,))
    return res
```

issue_body:

```
import numpy as np
import numexpr as ne
a = np.arange(10)
ne.evaluate('sum(a>5)')

```

issue_title: Make `AndroidDevice.take_bug_report` more useful
issue_body:
Right now, `AndroidDevice.take_bug_report` is meant to be used in functions like `BaseTestClass.on_fail`, because it requires a test name and a test begin time.

There's no reason to lock the usage of `AndroidDevice.take_bug_report` like this. We should generalize the usage to make it work in more situations.

issue_title: brotli extension not linked with libstdc++ on pypy3 for Linux
issue_body:
There is an issue with the Python 3-compatible pypy (aka pypy3) whereby distutils fails to use the proper C++ compiler/linker while compiling C++ extension modules (such as Brotli).

https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/1763/not-using-proper-c-compilers-linker-while

In the Python2.7-compatible pypy, they have fixed this by patching `distutils/unixccompiler.py` so that it uses `c++` as default C++ compiler instead of `cc`: 
https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/commits/c6e45dfbda905fa9e626782c8d2dd313ff3f54cf

However, they haven't ported this patch to pypy3 yet.

At [behdad/fonttools](https://github.com/behdad/fonttools), we use Brotli for WOFF2 and we test it on Travis under different python versions, including pypy3.

Because the C++ runtime library isn't being linked when compiling Brotli for pypy3, the module fails to be loaded with `undefined symbol` error.
For details, see: https://github.com/behdad/fonttools/issues/339

As far as I could test, this problem only occurs on **pypy3 for Linux** (the Travis python setup runs on Ubuntu 12.04). On **OS X**, where `distutils.unixccompiler` is also used, I verified that brotli is compiled and imported without problems when using the same pypy3 version (2.4.0) as the one used on Travis -- but I guess it's because on OSX the name `cc` is just a symbolic link to `clang`, and the latter does the right thing.

As a workaround, we currently do something like this:
```
python setup.py build_ext --libraries=stdc++
```

While pypy developers fixes the issue, Brotli's setup.py could be modified to link with libstdc++ by default whenever `platform.python_implementation() == "PyPy" and sys.version_info[0] == 3`. (I wonder if there would be portability issues if libstdc++ were linked all the time, on any python platform/version/implementation?)

A second alternative would be to change the value of `UnixCCompiler.compiler_cxx` variable from the current `['cc']` to `['c++']` if running on pypy3 (exactly like in the above mentioned pypy2 patch). This is what they do in [spaCy](https://github.com/honnibal/spaCy/blob/master/setup.py#L21) for example (I found this by chance).

Now, both approaches seem to work, though I'm not sure which one is preferable.

A third approach, of course, is simply take note and wait.

Anyway, I have mentioned this on pypy's bug tracker, where I found at least two duplicate issues ([#1099](https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/1099/distutils-not-correctly-using-g-cplusplus) and [#1763](https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/1763/not-using-proper-c-compilers-linker-while)) referencing the same problem.

Has anyone else encount
Cheers,

Cosimo

issue_title: sky scenes
issue_body:
I've been asked a few times recently whether GalSim has or will have some default "sky scenes", i.e., a way to decide on some galaxy or star sample to simulate rather than every user having to choose every single aspect of the sky scene themselves.  Obviously for specific applications people WILL want complete control over the galaxy/star sample they are simulating, but it seems like it might be valuable to have ~2-3 simple samples of objects to simulate.  For example:

- a star sample with a reasonable flux distribution in some optical band.
- a COSMOS-based galaxy sample.

One option is to simply put these into a demo to tell people to look at that code.

Another option is to make a "sky scene" module that returns a star catalog and a galaxy catalog that have the parameters needed to generate a reasonable sample.  It's obvious we can't make this infinitely customizable without having a gazillion parameters, which I think would be the argument for just putting it in a demo.  But I think that for example getting a COSMOS sample that is reasonable down to some limiting magnitude could be useful enough that it might be worth having a routine for it.  (I find myself often copying that code from the GREAT3 simulation scripts and thinking "why am I copying this again?!").

If there is some agreement on this, I'm likely to do this in #590 for use in the demo of WFIRST module capabilities as the last task before making a PR.

issue_title: Patroni does not detect extremely stale replicas
issue_body:
In the event a replica is very far behind, several of these errors will appear in the Postgres logs upon starting Patroni:

    FATAL:  could not receive data from WAL stream: ERROR:  requested WAL segment 00000005000000000000001B has already been removed

In the case a replica can not catch up to the primary node, Patroni should stop the replica and either rebuild the node, or quit attempting to rejoin the cluster.

issue_title: Proposed Tagline change
issue_body:
The current tag -- "Python Flask Framework to automagically handle REST API requests based on OpenAPI/Swagger"—doesn't completely convey all that Connexion does, or what makes it particularly useful. 

The tagline at Swagger.io—"A Swagger-first REST framework on top of Flask with validation and OAuth 2 support"—brings in the OAuth 2 support, which is good. 

But I'm wondering if the focus should be more along the lines of, "with Connexion, you write the Swagger spec, then it calls your Python code, handling the mapping from the specification to the code--not the other way around." In more concise language, obviously. :) Thoughts?

issue_title: Retirer le fichier model/datatrees
Gestion des changements de nomenclature des cases de la déclaration fiscale
issue_body:
Après avoir effectuer des tests sur la législation de l'impôt sur le revenu, il y a un problème qui revient souvent sur la définition de variables issues des déclarations d'impôt (2042). **Certaines cases de la déclaration fiscale changent de signification d'une année à l'autre.** 

- Soit la variable initiale que désignait cette case disparait et on reprend le nom de cette case pour désigner une nouvelle variable

Ex: _la case 7UH._
En 2002 : Investissements non professionnel réalisé par une entreprise dans les DOM-TOM
En 2009 : Versement sur un compte épargne codéveloppement
En 2016: Dons et cotisations versés aux partis politiques

- Soit la case englobe désormais plusieurs variables, précédemment définies dans des cases différentes

Ex: _les revenus industriels et commerciaux professionnels imposable (régime du bénéfice réel avec CGA ou viseur)._
Avant 2016 : 
revenus imposables (hors locations meublées) = cases 5KC, 5LC, 5MC => variable _abic_impn_
revenus imposables (locations meublées) =  cases 5HA, 5IA, 5JA => variable _abic_impm_
A partir de 2016 : 
revenus imposables (y compris locations meublées) = cases 5KC, 5LC, 5MC => quelle variable définir dans Openfisca-france ?

----------------

Etant donné qu'on ne peut plus définir de start_date, stop_date pour les variables, il me semble, il faudrait réfléchir à comment gérer ces changements de nomenclature au fil des années. **Plusieurs types de solutions différentes semblent coexister** pour l'instant, ce qui n'est pas forcement très lisible : 

- **Création de différentes variables avec nom de case + années, à partir des cerfa_field**
Ex : il existe une variable _f7uh_ et une variable _f7uh_2007_ (avec chacune un label différent) 
- **Création d'une seule variables avec nom de case à partir des cerfa_field, qui est ensuite appelée par différentes variables intermédiaires dont la formule dépend de l'année.**
Ex: _f7uh_ est utilisée pour calculer _dfppce_ à partir de 2013 (dons de financement de partis) mais aussi pour calculer _ecodev_ en 2009 (compte épargne codéveloppement). Le problème étant que _f7uh_ est labellisé "Dons et cotisations versés aux partis politiques" ...

issue_title: omego configuration file and command line parsing
issue_body:
I started work on this a while ago but it got sidetracked. I can think of two ways to do this:

1. Augment `argparse` with `ConfigFileParser` or similar
  - Example implementation (more or less works): https://github.com/manics/omego/compare/argparse_config_file
  - This requires two passes of the command line args:
    - On the first pass find and parse any config files specified on the command line (`-c FILE`, multiple allowed).
    - Use these parsed values as the default values in `argparse.add_argument`.
    - Do a full parse of the command line arguments
  - I've implemented this by subclassing the main argparse class (actually its a wrapper rather than a subclass to avoid relying on potentially non-public `_` members).
  - Advantages
    - if you specify a `type` in `add_argument` any values from a config file are converted
    - Doesn't require any major code changes.
    - Might be able to use [argcomplete](https://github.com/kislyuk/argcomplete) to generate bash-completions (though I've no idea whether it would work in practice with this modified argparse)
  - Disadvantages:
    - Disabling the early-exit-if-help for the first pass but not the second is a bit complicated
    - I'm not sure how robust extending argparse is with regards to future versions
    - If you specify an `action` arg to `add_argument` it might not be handled properly
  - Example: `omego upgrade -c omego-50-merge.cfg`

    ```
    # omego-50-merge.cfg
    [main]
    [db]
    [download]
    branch = OMERO-5.0-merge-ice35
    [install]
    branch = OMERO-5.0-merge-ice35
    [upgrade]
    branch = OMERO-5.0-merge-ice35
    [version]
    ```

2. Use [docopt](https://github.com/docopt/docopt)
  - Examples of docopt messages (note this is non-functional): https://github.com/manics/omego/compare/docopt_args
  - You write the help message, and it automatically creates a parser for you at runtime.
  - Advantages
    - It's cool :-)
    - Easier to understand
    - Relatively simple
  - Disadvantages:
    - No types, everything is a string
    - Gets more complicated because each command has it's own sub-parser
    - Merging of config file values and command line values would happen after command line parsing, and there's no simple way to distinguish between default values from one which is explicitly set to the same as a default value
    - A separate dependency (at present `omego` has no external deps for Python 2.7 which makes it easier to distribute), though we could bundle it since it's only one file docopt, or fork it to workaround the above limitations

If this is done correctly then it can go into [yaclifw](https://github.com/openmicroscopy/yaclifw)

issue_title: Indexing tools unable to handle large MAF files
issue_body:
One example is the hg38 projected chr1 multiz100way from UCSC: http://hgdownload.soe.ucsc.edu/goldenPath/hg38/multiz100way/maf/chr1.maf.gz (5.9G gz'd, 66G gunzip'd)

```
maf_build_index.py chr1.maf
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/path_to/bin/maf_build_index.py", line 83, in <module>
    if __name__ == "__main__": main()
  File "/path_to/bin/maf_build_index.py", line 80, in main
    indexes.write( out )
  File "/path_to/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bx/interval_index_file.py", line 332, in write
    write_packed( f, ">I", base )
  File "/path_to/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bx/interval_index_file.py", line 463, in write_packed
    f.write( pack( pattern, *vals ) )
struct.error: 'I' format requires 0 <= number <= 4294967295
```

One possibility is to up the version number and store unsigned integers as unsigned long long `>Q`, which would max out at 18446744073709551615 vs 4294967295. Would double the packed size though.

Another potential workaround could be to break the MAF up into multiple files, but I haven't tested this.

xref: https://biostar.usegalaxy.org/p/18196/

issue_title: makeotf: Within a ‘vkrn’ feature, value record format A should set YAdvance
issue_body:
The OpenType Feature File Specification says in [section 2.e.iv](http://www.adobe.com/devnet/opentype/afdko/topic_feature_file_syntax.html#2.e.iv) about Value Record Format A: “Here the ‹metric› represents an X advance adjustment, except when used in the ‘vkrn’ feature, in which case it represents a Y advance adjustment.”

However, with makeotf v2.0.90 of Nov 19 2015, the following feature file:
```
languagesystem DFLT dflt;
feature vkrn {
    position A -100;
} vkrn;
```

produces the following GPOS table when dumped with ttx:
```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<ttFont sfntVersion="true" ttLibVersion="3.0">

  <GPOS>
    <Version value="1.0"/>
    <ScriptList>
      <!-- ScriptCount=1 -->
      <ScriptRecord index="0">
        <ScriptTag value="DFLT"/>
        <Script>
          <DefaultLangSys>
            <ReqFeatureIndex value="65535"/>
            <!-- FeatureCount=1 -->
            <FeatureIndex index="0" value="0"/>
          </DefaultLangSys>
          <!-- LangSysCount=0 -->
        </Script>
      </ScriptRecord>
    </ScriptList>
    <FeatureList>
      <!-- FeatureCount=1 -->
      <FeatureRecord index="0">
        <FeatureTag value="vkrn"/>
        <Feature>
          <!-- LookupCount=1 -->
          <LookupListIndex index="0" value="0"/>
        </Feature>
      </FeatureRecord>
    </FeatureList>
    <LookupList>
      <!-- LookupCount=1 -->
      <Lookup index="0">
        <!-- LookupType=1 -->
        <LookupFlag value="0"/>
        <!-- SubTableCount=1 -->
        <SinglePos index="0" Format="1">
          <Coverage>
            <Glyph value="A"/>
          </Coverage>
          <ValueFormat value="4"/>
          <Value XAdvance="-100"/>
        </SinglePos>
      </Lookup>
    </LookupList>
  </GPOS>

</ttFont>
```

Instead of `<Value XAdvance="-100"/>`, shouldn’t this be `<Value YAdvance="-100"/>` since the value record format A appears inside a `vkrn` feature?

issue_title: JUnit Test Report file parsing fails when a test case is missing the "time" attribute
issue_body:
Following the upgrade to `1.3.0rc2`, we started receiving multiple errors while pants is trying to process the JUnit test result files.  The errors are of form:

```
Error parsing test result file /path/to/TEST-suite.xml: could not convert string to float
```

This is a regression from the previous version, `1.3.0.dev19`.  Upon investigating, it became apparent that every test suite with an error has at least one test case that lacks the `"time"` attribute in the test report.  For example, `TEST-mypackage.MyTest.xml` might have the following contents:

```
   ...
    <testcase classname="mypackage.MyTest" name="test1"/>
    <testcase classname="mypackage.MyTest" name="test2" time="0.001"/>
    <testcase classname="mypackage.MyTest" name="test3" time="0.004"/>
   ...
```

The error appears to be caused by the following commit: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/4561/files.  The problematic code appears to be on line 120 in `src/python/pants/task/testrunner_task_mixin.py`:

```
test_info = {'time': float(testcase.getAttribute('time'))}
```

The code doesn't check if the `"time"` attribute before converting it to a float.

issue_title: Strings that contain an integer starting with 0 are converted into integers
issue_body:
For instance, if you have a parameter in your template with a value that is a string "012345" it is silently converted into the integer 012345 after the transformation. 

This can raise issues:
- if the parameter is expecting a string as a value.
- if you use condition function to compare this values with other ones.
- if you use such a string as a key in a Mapping section (map key has to be strings).

Type tags, such as !!str, are not preserved by the transformation, so they cannot be used as a workaround.

issue_title: Multiprocessing works differently in Linux and Windows
issue_body:
Hi
That's my small example script. Create a subdirectory "metrics" in the folder from you are running the script and set the environment variable.
```
#!/usr/bin/python
'''
This is just an example of how to use prometheus_client in multiprocessing application
'''
import os
if 'prometheus_multiproc_dir' not in os.environ:
    os.environ['prometheus_multiproc_dir']='metrics'

from prometheus_client import Counter
from prometheus_client import start_http_server
from prometheus_client import CollectorRegistry
from prometheus_client.multiprocess import MultiProcessCollector
from prometheus_client import core
from multiprocessing import Pool


def worker(n):
    counter = Counter('counter', 'counter', registry=None)
    counter.inc()

def main():
    print("Main PID", os.getpid())
    clear_metrics_dir()
    registry = CollectorRegistry()
    MultiProcessCollector(registry)
    core.REGISTRY = registry
    start_http_server(8000)

    p = Pool(processes=10)
    p.map_async(worker, range(300000))

    p.close()
    p.join()
    print(registry.get_sample_value('counter'))


def clear_metrics_dir():
    dir = os.getcwd()
    os.chdir(os.environ['prometheus_multiproc_dir'])
    [os.remove(file) for file in os.listdir()]
    os.chdir(dir)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```
If I run this in Windows I got this output:
```
Main PID 27372
300000.0
```
and there are 10 files in the metrics subdirectory.

But on Linux the output is different:
```
Main PID 24671
42961.0
```
And there is only one file in the directory.

I'm not quite sure but my guess is the following:
In the core.py at lines 441-444 the variable _ValueClass is initialized:
```
if 'prometheus_multiproc_dir' in os.environ:
    _ValueClass = _MultiProcessValue()
else:
    _ValueClass = _MutexValue
```
And then that class is instantiated and it creates the files where the metrics data will be stored. 
The class itself is created in the function _MultiProcessValue() and it uses os.getpid() as a default value for the argument.
In Windows when you start another processes with _multiprocessing_ your main module is reloaded for each of the processes. And the prometheus_client library is also reloaded and the class is constructed for each of the new process.
In Lunux the module is loaded only once and therefore the class is created also only once. 
All that makes the library write all the metrics of all the processes to the same file on Linux! That makes the metrics basically wrong, and in the worst case the file got corrupted and the HTTP server can not read from the file. 
Though the stuff seems to work well if the load on the processes is small. E.g. if you put _sleep_ in the worker function and reduce the number of cycles it can (sometimes) work fine.

If i change the core.py in this way, it works fine on both Linux and Windows (lines 395-406):
```
def _MultiProcessValue(__pid=os.getpid()):
    files = {}
    files_lock = Lock()

    class _MmapedValue(object):
        '''A float protected by a mutex backed by a per-process mmaped file.'''

        _multiprocess = True

        def __init__(self, typ, metric_name, name, labelnames, labelvalues, multiprocess_mode='', **kwargs):
            pid = os.getpid()
            if typ == 'gauge':
```
but then the unit tests will most probably fail.

issue_title: Test cases for the zoo of non-spec compliant events
issue_body:
Sonos doesn't seem to make too much of an effort in making sure that the events that pertain to music service elements are DIDL-Lite spec compliant. This means that in order to allow these event through the events.py code, we will need to relax our implementation of the spec. This is annoying, as it makes it more difficult to ensure large changes in the code base against regressions with these wild events.

In order to fix this, we should collect as many examples of events that broke the code as possible and make test cases out of them.

The procedure for collection as test is the following:

 1. Install version 0.11 (before too many of the bug fixes pertaining to wild events) of SoCo and check that you can make events code crash
 2. In events.py change the lines:
```python
                    if value.startswith('<DIDL-Lite'):
                        value = from_didl_string(value)[0]
```
to
```python
                    if value.startswith('<DIDL-Lite'):
                        try:
                            value = from_didl_string(value)[0]
                        except:
                            with open('broken_event.txt', 'wb') as file_:
                                file_.write(value.encode('utf-8'))
                            raise
```
 3. That should write a utf-8 encoded version of the event that caused the code to break, to a file called `broken_event.txt` in the current directory. Attach that file to this issue (see instructions for that here: https://help.github.com/articles/file-attachments-on-issues-and-pull-requests/).

After we have collected the test cases, we should implement the functional tests for them. My ideas in that will follow. FIXME.

issue_title: Proxied replay prevents serviceWorker from functioning like on localhost
issue_body:
The ServiceWorker that is included in the ipwb replay system requires either HTTPS or localhost (potentially using HTTP) to function by design.

When using Proxy mode and HTTP on localhost, the ServiceWorker ceases to register or function.

For example, in my /etc/hosts file I added a line:
`127.0.0.1       myipwb`
...which should map all traffic for the "host" myipwb to localhost. I then run:

`ipwb index ipwb/samples/warcs/5mementos.warc | ipwb replay -P myipwb`

and access http://myipwb:5000 in my browser. The console reports:
"ServiceWorker registration failed: DOMException: Only secure origins are allowed (see: https://goo.gl/Y0ZkNV)."

This is expected because of the above but should be remedied or at least should provide the user with info of why their embedded mementos are not redirecting properly.

---
Also, with proxy mode, there may need to be port consideration when a proxied host is specified that does not contain this information. Perhaps 80/443 should be assumed or, as shown here, the default port propagated. This ticket is not about this facet.

issue_title: add more independencies in get_independencies of Bayesian Model
issue_body:
The `get_independencies` method should compute all the independencies present in the Bayesian network.  It should also include the local independencies given by the `local_independencies` as well.
```
[In] : from pgmpy.models import BayesianModel
[In] : G = BayesianModel()
[In] : G.add_edges_from([('D','G'), ('I','G'), ('G','L'), ('I','S')])
[In] : G.get_independencies()
[Out]: (S _|_ I, D | G)
           (S _|_ G, I, L | D)
           (S _|_ G, I, D | L)
           (G _|_ I, D, L | S)
           (G _|_ D, L | I)
           (G _|_ S, I, L | D)
           (G _|_ S, I, D | L)
           (I _|_ G, L | S)
           (I _|_ S, D | G)
           (I _|_ S, G, L | D)
           (I _|_ S, G, D | L)
           (D _|_ G, L | S)
           (D _|_ S, I | G)
           (D _|_ G, L | I)
           (D _|_ S, G, I | L)
           (L _|_ G, I, D | S)
           (L _|_ G, D | I)

if hasattr(asyncio, 'ensure_future'):
